clean_story:
Parents’ warnings. Offspring’s survival.

The term generational memory in insects describes how behaviors or preferences pass from one generation to the next—without learning, like a built-in instruction manual.

A mother butterfly doesn’t teach her caterpillars which leaves are safe. Instead, she lays eggs on the exact plant her own mother chose, because her body remembers the scent from her time as a larva. The cabbage white butterfly, for example, will reject mustard plants if their ancestors were poisoned by them—even if they’ve never tasted one themselves. This isn’t thought or choice; it’s chemistry. When a female wasp stings a caterpillar to paralyze it (not kill it), her venom carries traces of her mother’s past hunts, fine-tuning the dose for her own offspring.

Some ants inherit nest-building blueprints: Argentine ants raised in labs still dig tunnels with the same slope and chamber size as wild colonies, matching their ancestors’ designs from thousands of miles away. Scientists call this transgenerational epigenetic inheritance—where experiences alter genes temporarily, like a highlighter marking the most useful pages in a book.

Three related subjects are plant defenses against insects, how bees teach dance moves, and animal tools made without hands.
